title: Cirrhosis and Its Complications
authors: Alex S. Befeler, Bruce R. Bacon

items.title: Reversibility of Cirrhosis: Understanding Fibrosis Reversal
items.content: Cirrhosis was once thought to be irreversible, but recent findings suggest that fibrosis can reverse if the underlying cause is addressed. This reversal is notably observed in chronic hepatitis C treatment, iron removal in hemochromatosis, and cessation of alcohol use in alcohol-associated liver disease.
keyPoints: Cirrhosis can potentially reverse when the causative factor is removed.
Reversal of fibrosis has been documented with successful treatment of chronic hepatitis C.
Iron removal in hemochromatosis shows reversal of fibrosis.
Patients with alcohol-associated liver disease who stop drinking may experience fibrosis reversal.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis and Its Complications

items.title: Differentiating Cirrhosis: Stable vs. Decompensated
items.content: Clinicians must distinguish between stable, compensated cirrhosis and decompensated cirrhosis to guide management. Decompensated cirrhosis is characterized by complications such as ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, or variceal bleeding, which necessitate consideration for liver transplantation.
keyPoints: Advanced fibrosis includes bridging fibrosis with nodularity (stage 3) and cirrhosis (stage 4).
Decompensated cirrhosis features include ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, or variceal bleeding.
Patients with decompensated cirrhosis should be evaluated for liver transplantation if complications are poorly controlled.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis and Its Complications

items.title: Pathogenesis and Prevalence of Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis
items.content: Chronic alcohol use is a leading cause of liver disease, contributing to various forms including cirrhosis. In the U.S., it's the most commonly used drug with significant prevalence among adults, leading to substantial morbidity and mortality.
keyPoints: Alcohol is the most commonly used drug in the United States.
>70% of adults drink alcohol each year.
20% have had a binge within the past month.
>7% regularly consume more than four or five drinks five or more times a month.
>14 million U.S. adults meet criteria for alcohol use disorder.
Chronic liver disease is the tenth most common cause of death in adults.
~48% of deaths due to cirrhosis are from alcohol-associated cirrhosis.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Alcohol Metabolism and Liver Damage
items.content: Alcohol metabolism involves multiple pathways leading to liver damage through oxidative stress, acetaldehyde formation, and subsequent fibrosis. The processes include enzyme systems like ADH, MEOS, and catalase.
keyPoints: Ethanol is absorbed mainly by the small intestine.
Gastric alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) initiates metabolism.
Three liver enzyme systems: cytosolic ADH, microsomal ethanol oxidizing system (MEOS), peroxisomal catalase.
Acetaldehyde is a highly reactive molecule formed during metabolism.
Chronic use induces CYP2E1 in MEOS pathway, increasing reactive oxygen species and acetaldehyde production.
Acetaldehyde forms adducts with proteins/nucleic acids, disrupting enzyme activities and causing oxidative damage.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Features of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease
items.content: Diagnosis requires a detailed history of alcohol consumption, with symptoms ranging from nonspecific to severe complications. Physical examination and laboratory tests provide further diagnostic clues.
keyPoints: Accurate history: >2 drinks/day in women, >3 drinks/day in men for >12 months required.
Symptoms include right upper quadrant pain, fever, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, anorexia, malaise.
Complications: ascites, edema, GI hemorrhage, jaundice, encephalopathy.
Physical findings: enlarged liver/spleen, firm/nodular liver edge, scleral icterus, spider angiomas, parotid enlargement, digital clubbing, muscle wasting, edema, ascites.
Men may show decreased body hair, gynecomastia, testicular atrophy; women may have menstrual irregularities.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis
items.title: Diagnosis of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease
items.content: Patients with chronic alcohol consumption presenting clinical features, physical findings, or laboratory abnormalities should be considered for alcohol-associated liver disease. It is crucial to rule out other forms of chronic liver disease.
keyPoints: Prothrombin times are often prolonged and unresponsive to vitamin K in advanced disease.
Serum sodium levels can be depressed with ascites due to excess free water ingestion.
Elevated serum ALT and AST, typically <400 IU/mL, with AST > ALT by a 2:1 ratio in active drinkers.
Liver biopsy is not routinely performed unless alternative diagnosis suspected.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis
items.title: Management of Severe Alcoholic Hepatitis
items.content: Glucocorticoids may improve short-term survival in severe alcoholic hepatitis with a discriminant function (DF) >32. Treatment is discontinued if bilirubin does not improve after 7 days.
keyPoints: Discriminant Function (DF) = serum total bilirubin + (prothrombin time difference from control × 4.6).
Glucocorticoids used for DF >32; stop if no bilirubin improvement after 7 days.
Intravenous N-acetylcysteine with glucocorticoids may benefit short-term survival in DF >32.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis
items.title: Importance of Abstinence and Nutritional Support
items.content: Abstinence significantly improves prognosis in alcohol-associated liver disease. Adequate nutrition, including >21.5 kcal/kg body weight per day, is recommended for better survival.
keyPoints: Prognosis improved with abstinence; 50% 5-year survival if drinking continues.
Nutritional support: >21.5 kcal/kg/day associated with better survival.
Medications like acamprosate calcium and baclofen can reduce alcohol craving.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis

items.subtopic: CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C
items.title: Progression to Cirrhosis in Chronic Hepatitis C
items.content: Chronic hepatitis C infection often leads to cirrhosis over time, with a significant portion of those chronically infected developing this severe liver condition. The progression is immune-mediated and characterized by specific histological changes.
keyPoints: 80% of HCV-exposed patients develop chronic hepatitis C.
~20–30% of these will develop cirrhosis over 20–30 years.
In the US, ~5–6 million people have been exposed to HCV; ~4–5 million are chronically viremic.
Worldwide, ~170 million individuals have hepatitis C.
Liver damage in chronic hepatitis C is likely immune-mediated.
Histological features include portal-based fibrosis with bridging and nodularity.
Cirrhosis due to HCV presents as a mixed micro- and macronodular cirrhosis on biopsy.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C

items.subtopic: CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C
items.title: Progression to Cirrhosis in Chronic Hepatitis B
items.content: Chronic hepatitis B infection can lead to cirrhosis, with a notable percentage of those chronically infected developing this condition. Diagnosis involves specific serological markers and histological features.
keyPoints: 5% of adults exposed to hepatitis B develop chronic hepatitis B.
~20% of these patients will develop cirrhosis.
In the US, ~2 million are carriers of hepatitis B.
Worldwide, up to 15% may be infected in endemic areas; >300–400 million individuals have hepatitis B globally.
25% of those with chronic HBV infection may ultimately develop cirrhosis.
Diagnosis involves serologies: HBsAg, HBeAg, anti-HBe, and quantitative HBV DNA levels.
Histological findings include ground-glass hepatocytes indicating HBsAg presence.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C

items.subtopic: Cirrhosis due to Chronic Viral Hepatitis B
items.title: Why Antiviral Therapy is Crucial in Decompensated Cirrhosis from HBV
items.content: Antiviral therapy plays a pivotal role in managing decompensated liver disease caused by chronic hepatitis B. It effectively suppresses the virus, as evidenced by reduced amino-transferase and HBV DNA levels, while also improving histology through decreased inflammation and fibrosis.
keyPoints: Antiviral therapy can convert patients with decompensated liver disease to compensated states.
Preferred antivirals are entecavir and tenofovir due to their lower risk of viral resistance.
Interferon alpha is contraindicated in cirrhotic patients.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis due to Chronic Viral Hepatitis B or C | CIRRHOSIS FROM AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS AND METABOLIC DYSFUNCTION-ASSOCIATED STEATOTIC LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: CIRRHOSIS FROM AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS AND METABOLIC DYSFUNCTION-ASSOCIATED STEATOTIC LIVER DISEASE
items.title: How to Approach Cirrhosis in AIH and MASH Patients
items.content: In autoimmune hepatitis (AIH), patients with established cirrhosis often do not benefit from immunosuppressive therapy due to 'burned out' inflammation. Diagnosis relies on positive markers like ANA or ASMA. Conversely, active inflammation in cirrhotic AIH can respond well to immunosuppression. MASH-related cirrhosis is increasingly recognized as a progression of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease, often initially misdiagnosed as cryptogenic cirrhosis.
keyPoints: AIH patients with established cirrhosis typically do not benefit from immunosuppressive therapy due to lack of significant inflammation.
Diagnosis of AIH in cirrhotic patients requires positive autoimmune markers (ANA or ASMA).
Active inflammation in AIH can respond to immunosuppression.
MASH is a common cause of previously cryptogenic cirrhosis, often progressing from metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis due to Chronic Viral Hepatitis B or C | CIRRHOSIS FROM AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS AND METABOLIC DYSFUNCTION-ASSOCIATED STEATOTIC LIVER DISEASE

items.title: Differentiating Intrahepatic vs Extrahepatic Biliary Cirrhosis for Therapeutic Decisions
items.content: In biliary cirrhosis, distinguishing between intrahepatic and extrahepatic causes is crucial because it directly influences the therapeutic approach. Extrahepatic obstruction can often be managed with surgical or endoscopic decompression of the biliary tract, whereas intrahepatic cholestasis does not benefit from these interventions.
keyPoints: Intrahepatic and extrahepatic categories are based on anatomic sites of bile retention.
Extrahepatic obstruction may be treated with surgical or endoscopic decompression.
Intrahepatic cholestatic processes require different therapeutic approaches.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · BILIARY CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Common Causes and Histopathologic Features of Chronic Cholestatic Syndromes
items.content: Chronic cholestatic syndromes, such as primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), autoimmune cholangitis (AIC), primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), and idiopathic adulthood ductopenia, are distinguished by antibody testing, cholangiographic findings, and clinical presentation. They share histopathologic features including cholate stasis, copper deposition, xanthomatous transformation of hepatocytes, biliary fibrosis, chronic portal inflammation, interface activity, and chronic lobular inflammation.
keyPoints: Major causes include PBC, AIC, PSC, and idiopathic adulthood ductopenia.
Distinguished by antibody testing, cholangiographic findings, and clinical presentation.

Shared histopathologic features: cholate stasis, copper deposition, xanthomatous transformation of hepatocytes, biliary fibrosis, chronic portal inflammation, interface activity, chronic lobular inflammation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · BILIARY CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Markers and Overlap Syndrome in PBC
items.content: Antimitochondrial antibodies (AMAs) are present in approximately 95% of patients with Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC), serving as a crucial diagnostic marker. However, up to 10% of patients may exhibit features of autoimmune hepatitis (AIH), known as 'overlap' syndrome, requiring similar treatment approaches.
keyPoints: AMAs are present in ~95% of PBC cases and are key diagnostic markers.
Overlap syndrome occurs in up to 10% of PBC patients, characterized by moderate to severe interphase hepatitis on biopsy, elevated ALT >5x ULN, and elevated IgG levels.
Patients with overlap syndrome are usually treated as typical PBC patients.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PRIMARY BILIARY CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Prognosis of Pruritus in PBC
items.content: Pruritus is a common symptom in PBC, affecting about 50% of patients at diagnosis. It can be severe and debilitating, often worsening in the evening. Pruritus occurring before jaundice suggests more advanced disease and a poorer prognosis.
keyPoints: Pruritus affects ~50% of PBC patients at diagnosis and is often worse in the evening.
Intermittent pruritus can be mistaken for cholestasis of pregnancy if it develops late in gestation.
Pruritus before jaundice indicates severe disease and a poor prognosis.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PRIMARY BILIARY CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Laboratory Findings and Diagnostic Approach for PBC
items.content: PBC is characterized by cholestatic liver enzyme abnormalities, including elevated gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase and alkaline phosphatase. Immunoglobulin M levels are typically increased. Liver biopsy remains crucial in AMA-negative cases to differentiate from primary sclerosing cholangitis.
keyPoints: Cholestatic liver enzyme abnormalities include elevated gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase and ALP, with mild elevations in ALT and AST.
IgM levels are typically increased in PBC patients.
Liver biopsy is essential for diagnosis in AMA-negative cases to rule out primary sclerosing cholangitis.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PRIMARY BILIARY CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Ursodeoxycholic Acid in PBC: Indications and Limitations
items.content: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) is effective for improving biochemical and histologic features of primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), especially when initiated early. However, its efficacy diminishes in patients presenting with cirrhosis manifestations.
keyPoints: UDCA improves both biochemical and histologic features of PBC.
Initiation of UDCA therapy is most effective before the development of cirrhosis manifestations.
Doses: 13–15 mg/kg per day.
Side effects may include worsening pruritus, diarrhea, or headache.

30-40% of patients do not respond satisfactorily to UDCA; alternative treatments like obeticholic acid, elafibranor, or seladelpar are considered for non-responders but should be avoided in cirrhosis with portal hypertension or decompensation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Biliary Cholangitis

items.title: Management of Symptoms and Complications in PBC
items.content: Fatigue is a primary symptom of PBC, but no specific therapy has proven effective; naps are recommended. Pruritus can be managed with antihistamines, naltrexone, SSRIs, rifampin, or cholestyramine. Patients should also be monitored for osteopenia/osteoporosis and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies.
keyPoints: Fatigue is a main symptom; no effective specific therapy exists; recommend frequent naps.
Pruritus management options include antihistamines, naltrexone, SSRIs, rifampin, cholestyramine, and plasmapheresis for severe cases.
Increased risk of osteopenia/osteoporosis in PBC patients; bone density testing recommended.
Oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation advised.
Bisphosphonate treatment for identified bone disease.
Screening for fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies (A, D, E, K) if total bilirubin >2 mg/dL.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Biliary Cholangitis

items.title: Diagnosis of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
items.content: The diagnosis of PSC is primarily established through imaging techniques, as liver biopsy changes are not pathognomonic. MRI with MRCP is the preferred initial imaging method due to its non-invasive nature and detailed visualization capabilities.
keyPoints: Liver biopsy changes in PSC include bile duct proliferation, ductopenia, fibrous cholangitis (pericholangitis), and periductal fibrosis.
MRI with MRCP is the imaging technique of choice for initial evaluation.
ERCP can be considered if MRCP images are suboptimal or if there's newly elevated total bilirubin or evidence of a dominant stricture on MRCP.
Cholangiographic findings in PSC include multifocal stricturing and beading involving both intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary tree.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PRIMARY SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Clinical Features and Laboratory Findings of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
items.content: Patients with PSC present with clinical features typical of cholestatic liver disease, including fatigue, pruritus, and steatorrhea. Laboratory findings often reveal elevated ALP levels, sometimes more than twofold the normal value, along with possible elevations in aminotransferases.
keyPoints: Clinical features include fatigue, pruritus (not correlated with disease severity), steatorrhea, and deficiencies of fat-soluble vitamins.
Laboratory findings typically show at least a twofold increase in ALP; some patients may have elevated aminotransferases.
~65% of PSC patients test positive for perinuclear antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (pANCA).
60-80% of PSC patients have inflammatory bowel disease, predominantly ulcerative colitis.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PRIMARY SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.title: Management of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
items.content: There is no specific proven treatment for PSC. Clinicians may use UDCA at dosages similar to those used in primary biliary cholangitis (13–15 mg/kg per day) with anecdotal benefits, but high-dose UDCA (28–30 mg/kg per day) has been found harmful. Endoscopic dilatation of dominant strictures can be beneficial, and liver transplantation is the definitive treatment when decompensated cirrhosis develops or in cases of recurrent cholangitis.
keyPoints: No proven specific treatment for PSC
UDCA used at 13–15 mg/kg per day with anecdotal improvement
High-dose UDCA (28–30 mg/kg per day) is harmful
Endoscopic dilatation of strictures can be helpful
Liver transplantation indicated in decompensated cirrhosis or recurrent cholangitis
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis | CARDIAC CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Understanding Cardiac Cirrhosis and Its Clinical Features
items.content: Cardiac cirrhosis results from long-standing right-sided congestive heart failure, leading to chronic liver injury. It is characterized by elevated ALP levels with normal or slightly increased aminotransferases (AST usually higher than ALT). Patients typically present with signs of congestive heart failure and an enlarged firm liver on examination. Variceal hemorrhage or encephalopathy are unlikely.
keyPoints: Result of long-standing right-sided congestive heart failure
Elevated ALP, normal/slightly increased aminotransferases (AST > ALT)
Physical exam reveals enlarged firm liver
Variceal hemorrhage or encephalopathy unlikely
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis | CARDIAC CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Hemochromatosis: Understanding Prevalence and Management
items.content: Hemochromatosis is a common genetic disorder of iron metabolism that can lead to cirrhosis, liver failure, and hepatocellular cancer. Despite its prevalence, only a small percentage develop severe liver disease.
keyPoints: Genetic susceptibility occurs in 1 in 250 individuals.
<5% of genotypically susceptible patients will develop severe liver disease from hemochromatosis.
Diagnosis involves elevated transferrin saturation and ferritin levels, confirmed by HFE mutation analysis.
Treatment is effective with regular therapeutic phlebotomy.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · OTHER TYPES OF CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Wilson's Disease: Early Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Wilson's disease is a rare inherited disorder affecting copper metabolism, leading to liver damage. Early diagnosis can prevent irreversible end-stage manifestations.
keyPoints: Affects 1 in 30,000 individuals, typically adolescents and young adults.
Diagnosis requires low ceruloplasmin levels, elevated 24-h urine copper levels, Kayser-Fleischer corneal rings, and liver biopsy findings.
Treatment involves copper-chelating medications.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · OTHER TYPES OF CIRRHOSIS

items.subtopic: MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION
items.title: Understanding Portal Hypertension in Cirrhosis: Definition and Pathophysiology
items.content: Portal hypertension is defined by an elevation of the hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) to >5 mmHg. It results from increased intrahepatic resistance due to cirrhosis, regenerative nodules, and microthrombi, alongside increased splanchnic blood flow due to vasodilation in the splanchnic vascular bed.
keyPoints: Portal hypertension is defined as HVPG >5 mmHg.
Causes include increased intrahepatic resistance and increased splanchnic blood flow.
Neurohumoral responses contribute to sodium retention, increased blood volume, and hyperdynamic circulation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION
items.title: Clinical Significance of Clinically Significant Portal Hypertension (CSPH)
items.content: Clinically significant portal hypertension is defined as HVPG ≥10 mmHg. It indicates a high risk for decompensation, leading to complications like variceal bleeding, ascites, or hepatic encephalopathy.
keyPoints: CSPH is defined by an HVPG of ≥10 mmHg.
Risk of decompensation with variceal bleeding, ascites, or hepatic encephalopathy increases significantly.
Median mortality post-decompensation is <2 years.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION
items.title: Diagnosis and Risk Factors for Esophageal Variceal Hemorrhage in Cirrhosis
items.content: Esophageal varices are a major complication of portal hypertension, with 20-30% mortality per bleeding episode. Screening endoscopy is common to identify varices in cirrhotic patients, as one-third have varices and 5-15% develop them annually.
keyPoints: Esophageal variceal hemorrhage has a 20–30% mortality rate per episode.
Approximately one-third of cirrhotics have esophageal varices on screening endoscopy.
Risk factors for bleeding include severity of cirrhosis, size and location of varices, and presence of tense ascites.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION

items.title: Primary Prophylaxis of Variceal Bleeding in Cirrhosis
items.content: Prophylactic strategies are crucial for patients with cirrhosis to prevent variceal bleeding. Surveillance endoscopy is recommended at diagnosis and periodically based on disease activity. Nonselective beta-blockers (NSBBs) like propranolol or nadolol, and carvedilol, are first-line treatments to reduce portal pressure and prevent bleeding. Carvedilol is preferred due to its additional benefits in lowering portal pressure without heart rate goals.
keyPoints: Endoscopy recommended at diagnosis of compensated cirrhosis; every 2 years if active or every 3 years if inactive (with alcohol cessation, viral hepatitis eradication).
NSBBs like propranolol or nadolol target a heart rate of 55–60 bpm with systolic BP >90 mmHg.
Carvedilol preferred for primary prophylaxis due to additional anti-α-adrenergic vasodilating properties and potential benefits in preventing hepatic decompensation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Variceal Hemorrhage

items.title: Management of Acute Variceal Bleeding
items.content: Acute variceal bleeding is life-threatening and requires immediate management. Fluid and red blood cell replacement stabilize hemodynamics, with a transfusion strategy aiming for hemoglobin levels between 7–9 g/dL to reduce rebleeding and mortality. Vasoconstricting agents like somatostatin or octreotide improve initial control of bleeding. Prophylactic antibiotics such as ceftriaxone are recommended before endoscopy to prevent infections, recurrent bleeding, and mortality.
keyPoints: Transfusion strategy: hemoglobin goal 7–9 g/dL reduces early rebleeding and mortality.
Vasoconstricting agents (somatostatin or octreotide) improve initial bleeding control and reduce transfusion needs and mortality.
Prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) before endoscopy decrease infections, recurrent bleeding, and mortality.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Variceal Hemorrhage

items.title: Role of TIPS in Variceal Bleeding Management
items.content: Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) is used when endoscopic therapy fails or for high-risk patients. It creates a portocaval shunt to reduce portal pressure and prevent rebleeding. However, TIPS can lead to encephalopathy in up to 20% of patients. Patients with recurrent variceal bleeding after initial treatment should be considered for TIPS as long-term prevention.
keyPoints: TIPS is reserved for endoscopic failure or high-risk patients; encephalopathy occurs in ~20% of cases post-TIPS.
Consider TIPS for long-term prevention in recurrent variceal bleeding.
Patients undergoing TIPS should be evaluated for liver transplantation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Variceal Hemorrhage

items.subtopic: PORTAL HYPERTENSIVE GASTROPATHY | SPLENOMEGALY AND HYPERSPLENISM | ASCITES
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Portal Hypertensive Gastropathy
items.content: Portal hypertensive gastropathy is associated with portal hypertension and can cause both acute GI bleeding and chronic iron-deficiency anemia. It is diagnosed by endoscopy, revealing a snakeskin-like mosaic pattern on the gastric mucosa with central red or brown spots.
keyPoints: Endoscopic findings: snakeskin-like mosaic pattern with central red/brown spots
Treatment for bleeding includes NSBB and iron repletion; refractory cases may respond to TIPS
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PORTAL HYPERTENSIVE GASTROPATHY | SPLENOMEGALY AND HYPERSPLENISM | ASCITES

items.subtopic: PORTAL HYPERTENSIVE GASTROPATHY | SPLENOMEGALY AND HYPERSPLENISM | ASCITES
items.title: Clinical Features of Splenomegaly and Hypersplenism in Portal Hypertension
items.content: Splenomegaly with hypersplenism is often the first sign of portal hypertension, characterized by an enlarged spleen on examination and thrombocytopenia/leukopenia. Some patients may experience left-sided abdominal pain.
keyPoints: First indication of portal hypertension
Thrombocytopenia and leukopenia in cirrhotic patients
Splenomegaly usually requires no specific treatment
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PORTAL HYPERTENSIVE GASTROPATHY | SPLENOMEGALY AND HYPERSPLENISM | ASCITES

items.subtopic: PORTAL HYPERTENSIVE GASTROPATHY | SPLENOMEGALY AND HYPERSPLENISM | ASCITES
items.title: Diagnosis and Pathogenesis of Ascites in Cirrhosis
items.content: Ascites, primarily caused by portal hypertension due to cirrhosis, involves increased splanchnic lymph production from intrahepatic resistance and vasodilation. Diagnosis is via physical exam and imaging; diagnostic paracentesis includes protein, albumin, cell counts, cultures, amylase, and cytology.
keyPoints: Most common cause: portal hypertension due to cirrhosis
Pathogenesis involves increased splanchnic lymph production
Diagnostic paracentesis recommended with SAAG >1.1 g/dL indicating portal hypertension-related ascites
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PORTAL HYPERTENSIVE GASTROPATHY | SPLENOMEGALY AND HYPERSPLENISM | ASCITES

items.title: Management of Ascites: Dietary Sodium Restriction and Diuretics
items.content: For patients with small amounts of ascites, dietary sodium restriction is often sufficient. Most diets in the U.S. contain 6–8 g of sodium per day; reducing intake to 2 g/day (equivalent to slightly more than three-quarters of a teaspoon of salt) can be challenging but crucial. When moderate ascites persists despite dietary measures, diuretic therapy with spironolactone and furosemide is recommended.
keyPoints: Dietary sodium restriction alone may manage small amounts of ascites.
Average U.S. diets contain 6–8 g of sodium per day; aim for <2 g/day.
Start diuretic therapy with spironolactone (100 mg/d) and furosemide (40 mg/d).
Increase spironolactone to a maximum of 400 mg/d and furosemide to 160 mg/d if needed.
Failure of diuretics may indicate non-compliance with low-sodium diet.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Ascites

items.title: Management of Refractory Ascites: LVP and TIPS Considerations
items.content: In patients with large amounts of ascites despite compliance with a low-sodium diet, refractory ascites is diagnosed. Treatment options include repeated large-volume paracentesis (LVP) or the Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS) procedure. After LVP of ≥5 L, intravenous 25% albumin at 6–8 g/L should be administered to prevent circulatory dysfunction.
keyPoints: Refractory ascites requires alternative treatments like LVP or TIPS.
Administer IV 25% albumin at 6–8 g/L after LVP of ≥5 L.
TIPS does not improve survival and may increase hepatic encephalopathy risk.
Consider liver transplantation for patients with poor prognosis.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Ascites

items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
items.content: SBP is a critical complication in cirrhotic patients with ascites, characterized by infection without an intraabdominal source. Diagnosis hinges on detecting an absolute neutrophil count >250/μL in the ascitic fluid. Immediate treatment involves intravenous third-generation cephalosporin for 5 days and intravenous albumin to mitigate renal failure risks.
keyPoints: SBP occurs in up to 30% of hospitalized cirrhotic patients with ascites.
In-hospital mortality rate is approximately 25%.
Bacterial translocation from the gut is the presumed mechanism.
Common organisms include Escherichia coli and other gut bacteria; gram-positive bacteria can also be involved.
Diagnosis requires an absolute neutrophil count >250/μL in ascitic fluid.
Treatment includes intravenous third-generation cephalosporin for 5 days.
Intravenous albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1.0 g/kg on day 3) reduces renal failure risk and improves survival.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.title: Prophylaxis for Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
items.content: Cirrhotic patients with variceal hemorrhage or a history of SBP are at increased risk and require prophylactic measures. Prophylaxis is recommended during upper GI bleeding, and quinolone antibiotics should be used post-recovery to prevent recurrence.
keyPoints: SBP frequency increases significantly in patients with variceal hemorrhage.
Prophylaxis against SBP is advised at presentation with upper GI bleeding.
Quinolone antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for patients who have recovered from an episode of SBP to prevent recurrence.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of HRS-AKI
items.content: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) is a form of functional renal failure seen in about 10% of patients with advanced cirrhosis or acute liver failure. It involves marked disturbances in arterial renal circulation, characterized by increased vascular resistance and reduced systemic vascular resistance. Diagnosis of HRS-AKI requires exclusion of other causes of AKI, such as volume depletion and intrinsic kidney diseases. Initial management includes stopping diuretics and administering albumin infusion (1 g/kg per day for 48 hours) without significant improvement before considering vasoconstrictors.
keyPoints: HRS occurs in ~10% of patients with advanced cirrhosis or acute liver failure.
Diagnosis involves a stepwise increase in creatinine and exclusion of other AKI causes.
Acute kidney injury (AKI) is defined by a 0.3-mg/dL rise in creatinine over 48 hours or a 50% rise from baseline.
Initial management includes stopping diuretics and administering albumin infusion (1 g/kg per day for 48 hours).
Vasoconstrictors like terlipressin or low-dose norepinephrine are used if no improvement with albumin.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATORENAL SYNDROME

items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of HRS
items.content: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) is divided into two types: HRS-AKI, characterized by rapid renal function impairment, and HRS-CKD, with stable reduced glomerular filtration rate. Treatment for HRS involves vasoconstrictors such as terlipressin or low-dose norepinephrine under intensive care monitoring. If these treatments fail, renal replacement therapy may be initiated. The best treatment is liver transplantation, which typically results in recovery of renal function. Prognosis remains poor without transplantation.
keyPoints: HRS-AKI involves rapid impairment in renal function; HRS-CKD has stable reduced glomerular filtration rate.
Vasoconstrictors like terlipressin or low-dose norepinephrine are primary treatments, requiring ICU monitoring.
Renal replacement therapy is considered if vasoconstrictor treatment fails.
Liver transplantation is the best treatment and typically results in renal function recovery.
Prognosis for HRS patients is poor without liver transplantation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATORENAL SYNDROME

items.title: Understanding Hepatic Encephalopathy: Mechanisms and Clinical Features
items.content: Hepatic encephalopathy is a complication of chronic liver disease characterized by altered mental status due to liver failure. It results from gut-derived neurotoxins, such as ammonia, reaching the brain because of vascular shunting and decreased hepatic mass. In acute liver failure, encephalopathy can lead to life-threatening brain edema and cerebral herniation.
keyPoints: Hepatic encephalopathy is more common in chronic liver disease than acute liver failure.
Gut-derived neurotoxins like ammonia contribute to symptoms but are poorly correlated with disease severity.
In acute liver failure, encephalopathy can cause brain edema and cerebral herniation.
Treatment for brain edema includes hypertonic saline or mannitol.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.title: Diagnosing and Managing Hepatic Encephalopathy in Cirrhosis
items.content: In cirrhotic patients, hepatic encephalopathy often results from precipitating events such as volume depletion or infection. Clinical features include confusion, personality changes, and asterixis. Diagnosis is clinical, requiring experienced clinicians to recognize symptoms and rule out alternative causes.
keyPoints: Precipitating factors for encephalopathy in cirrhosis include GI bleeding, hyponatremia, and constipation.
Asterixis, or 'liver flap,' is a key clinical sign of hepatic encephalopathy.
Management involves ruling out infection, correcting electrolytes, and addressing precipitating factors.
Diagnosis relies on clinical assessment rather than ammonia levels.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.title: Management of Hepatic Encephalopathy: Focus on Lactulose and Rifaximin
items.content: The primary treatment for hepatic encephalopathy involves the use of lactulose to promote colonic acidification, aiming for two to three soft stools per day. For recurrent episodes or when lactulose is insufficient, rifaximin (550 mg twice daily) is effective in preventing recurrence.
keyPoints: Lactulose therapy aims for 2-3 soft stools/day.
Rifaximin dose: 550 mg twice daily.
Rifaximin replaces neomycin/metronidazole due to toxicity concerns.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Hepatic Encephalopathy | ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE

items.title: ACLF: Recognition, Management, and Prognosis
items.content: Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) involves acute decompensation of cirrhosis with multiorgan failure. It is precipitated by direct liver injury or systemic effects like infections. Mortality ranges from 20 to 70% at 28 days, increasing with organ failures. Management includes treating precipitating causes and considering ICU care or liver transplantation.
keyPoints: ACLF characterized by acute decompensation of cirrhosis with multiorgan failure.
Mortality at 28 days: 20-70%, increases with more organ failures.
Consider ICU care and liver transplantation; transition to palliative care if no improvement after 3-7 days.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Hepatic Encephalopathy | ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS)
items.content: Hepatopulmonary syndrome is characterized by arterial hypoxemia in cirrhotic patients without significant lung disease, due to intrapulmonary vascular dilations causing blood shunting. It presents with dyspnea and platypnea.
keyPoints: Occurs in 4–32% of patients with cirrhosis
Diagnosis involves hypoxemia demonstration and bubble echocardiography for shunt detection
Treatment includes oxygen supplementation and liver transplantation
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · LIVER-LUNG SYNDROMES | MALNUTRITION IN CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Nutritional Management in Cirrhosis to Prevent Sarcopenia
items.content: Patients with decompensated cirrhosis often develop malnutrition and sarcopenia due to increased catabolism and decreased caloric intake. Guidelines recommend nutritional education, periodic assessments, and early referrals for personalized therapeutic plans.
keyPoints: Caloric intake recommendation: 35 kcal/kg per day based on actual weight
Protein intake recommendation: 1.2–1.5 g/kg per day based on ideal body weight
Exercise recommendations include 150–300 min of aerobic exercise weekly and muscle-strengthening exercises twice a week
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · LIVER-LUNG SYNDROMES | MALNUTRITION IN CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Coagulation Disorders in Cirrhosis: Clinical Judgment Over Laboratory Tests
items.content: In cirrhotic patients, traditional laboratory measures like prothrombin time and international normalized ratio are unreliable for assessing coagulation status. The balance between pro- and anticoagulant factors is delicate, with potential shifts toward thrombosis or bleeding due to decreased synthesis of these factors. Thrombocytopenia may arise from hypersplenism linked to portal hypertension, but this can be offset by increased von Willebrand factor levels. Adequate thrombin formation is possible when platelet counts exceed 50,000–60,000/L. Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors are reduced in cholestatic syndromes due to impaired bile flow, which affects vitamin K absorption; however, intravenous or intramuscular vitamin K can quickly address this issue. Clinicians should assess coagulation status based on clinical judgment rather than laboratory tests alone.
keyPoints: Prothrombin time and international normalized ratio are not reliable indicators of clotting ability in cirrhosis.
Thrombocytopenia may occur due to hypersplenism, but increased von Willebrand factor can counterbalance this effect.
Adequate thrombin formation is possible with platelet levels >50,000–60,000/L.
Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X are diminished in cholestatic syndromes due to impaired bile flow.
Intravenous or intramuscular vitamin K can quickly correct coagulation abnormalities related to vitamin K deficiency.
Clinical judgment is preferred over laboratory tests for assessing coagulation status in cirrhotic patients.
Standard prophylaxis for deep venous thrombosis is generally recommended when hospitalized.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS

items.title: Osteoporosis Management in Cirrhosis: Role of DEXA and Bisphosphonates
items.content: Cirrhotic patients, particularly those with chronic cholestatic liver disease, frequently develop osteoporosis due to malabsorption of vitamin D and reduced calcium intake. This condition arises as bone resorption outpaces new bone formation. Dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) is an effective tool for diagnosing osteoporosis or osteopenia in these patients. When a DEXA scan confirms osteoporosis, treatment with bisphosphonates has been shown to be beneficial.
keyPoints: Osteoporosis is common in cirrhotic patients due to malabsorption of vitamin D and decreased calcium intake.
Bone resorption exceeds new bone formation in cirrhosis, leading to bone loss.
Dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) is useful for diagnosing osteoporosis or osteopenia.
Bisphosphonates are effective in treating osteoporosis confirmed by DEXA scan.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS

items.question: What is the best therapy for HRS?

Use of midodrine and octreotide as first-line treatments.

items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATORENAL SYNDROME

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical precipitating event for hepatic encephalopathy in patients with cirrhosis?
items.explanation: Hyponatremia, not hypernatremia, is a precipitating event for hepatic encephalopathy.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.question: What clinical sign can be elicited by having patients extend their arms and bend their wrists back in cases of hepatic encephalopathy?
items.explanation: Asterixis, or 'liver flap', is the clinical sign elicited in hepatic encephalopathy.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.question: Which treatment is used to decrease brain edema in acute liver failure?
items.explanation: Mannitol, along with hypertonic saline, is used to decrease brain edema in acute liver failure.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY

items.question: Which of the following is the mainstay treatment for hepatic encephalopathy?
items.explanation: The mainstay of treatment for encephalopathy is lactulose, which results in colonic acidification and helps eliminate nitrogenous products.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Hepatic Encephalopathy | ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE

items.question: Which antibiotic has replaced neomycin and metronidazole for preventing recurrent hepatic encephalopathy due to lower toxicity?
items.explanation: Rifaximin has replaced neomycin and metronidazole for preventing recurrent encephalopathy due to its lower toxicity.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Hepatic Encephalopathy | ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE

items.question: What is the recommended daily stool frequency goal when using lactulose therapy for hepatic encephalopathy?
options: Two to three soft stools per day
Four to five soft stools per day
items.explanation: The goal of lactulose therapy is to promote two to three soft stools per day.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Hepatic Encephalopathy | ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of hepatopulmonary syndrome (HPS) in patients with cirrhosis?
options: Arterial hypoxemia without significant lung disease
items.explanation: HPS is characterized by arterial hypoxemia in patients with cirrhosis and no significant underlying lung disease.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · LIVER-LUNG SYNDROMES | MALNUTRITION IN CIRRHOSIS

items.question: What diagnostic method is used to confirm portopulmonary hypertension (PPHT) in patients with cirrhosis?
items.explanation: Diagnosis of PPHT is confirmed by right heart catheterization showing elevated mean pulmonary artery pressure and other specific hemodynamic criteria.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · LIVER-LUNG SYNDROMES | MALNUTRITION IN CIRRHOSIS

items.question: Which condition in cirrhosis patients manifests as a loss of muscle mass and function, leading to frailty?
items.explanation: Sarcopenia is the condition characterized by loss of muscle mass and function, leading to frailty in cirrhosis patients.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · LIVER-LUNG SYNDROMES | MALNUTRITION IN CIRRHOSIS

items.subtopic: ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS
items.question: Which of the following is a reason why typical clinical measures of coagulation are not reliable in cirrhotic patients?
options: There is decreased synthesis of both pro- and anticoagulant factors.
Prothrombin time and international normalized ratio accurately reflect clotting ability.
Patients with cirrhosis always have adequate thrombin formation regardless of platelet levels.
Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors are unaffected in chronic cholestatic syndromes.
items.explanation: Decreased synthesis of both pro- and anticoagulant factors leads to unreliable clinical measures of coagulation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS

items.subtopic: ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS
items.question: What is a common hematologic abnormality in red blood cell morphology seen in patients with chronic liver disease?
items.explanation: Macrocytosis is a common abnormality in red blood cell morphology seen in patients with chronic liver disease.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS

items.subtopic: ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS
items.question: What is a common cause of osteoporosis in patients with chronic cholestatic liver disease?
options: Malabsorption of vitamin D and decreased calcium ingestion
items.explanation: Osteoporosis is common due to malabsorption of vitamin D and decreased calcium ingestion.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ABNORMALITIES IN COAGULATION | BONE DISEASE IN CIRRHOSIS | HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES IN CIRRHOSIS

items.statement: Patients with stable, compensated cirrhosis have the same clinical management as those with decompensated cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Patients with decompensated cirrhosis require specific therapy and consideration for liver transplantation.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis and Its Complications

items.statement: The complications of cirrhosis are the same regardless of etiology.
items.explanation: Complications such as portal hypertension, ascites, and variceal bleeding occur similarly across different causes.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis and Its Complications

items.statement: Acetaldehyde is metabolized to acetate by aldehyde dehydrogenase.
items.explanation: Ultimately, acetaldehyde is metabolized to acetate by aldehyde dehydrogenase.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: The majority of ethanol oxidation occurs via the MEOS pathway.
items.explanation: Normally, the majority of ethanol oxidation occurs via ADH.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis
items.statement: Serum sodium levels are usually elevated in patients with alcohol-associated cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Serum sodium levels are usually normal unless patients have ascites, then they can be depressed due to ingestion of excess free water.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis
items.statement: Liver biopsy is routinely performed in all patients with suspected alcohol-associated liver disease.
items.explanation: Liver biopsy can be helpful to confirm a diagnosis but generally is not performed unless there is suspicion of an alternative diagnosis.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED CIRRHOSIS (part 2) | Alcohol-Associated Cirrhosis and Alcoholic Hepatitis

items.subtopic: CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C
items.statement: HCV is a cytopathic virus.

items.subtopic: CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C
items.statement: Ground-glass hepatocytes are indicative of HBsAg presence in hepatitis B.
items.explanation: The presence of ground-glass hepatocytes signifies the presence of HBs antigen (HBsAg) in hepatitis B.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · CIRRHOSIS DUE TO CHRONIC VIRAL HEPATITIS B OR C

items.subtopic: Cirrhosis due to Chronic Viral Hepatitis B or C | CIRRHOSIS FROM AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS AND METABOLIC DYSFUNCTION-ASSOCIATED STEATOTIC LIVER DISEASE
items.statement: Patients with decompensated liver disease from hepatitis B can become compensated with antiviral therapy.
items.explanation: Clinical trials and case series have shown that patients with decompensated liver disease due to hepatitis B can improve with antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Cirrhosis due to Chronic Viral Hepatitis B or C | CIRRHOSIS FROM AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS AND METABOLIC DYSFUNCTION-ASSOCIATED STEATOTIC LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: Cirrhosis due to Chronic Viral Hepatitis B or C | CIRRHOSIS FROM AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS AND METABOLIC DYSFUNCTION-ASSOCIATED STEATOTIC LIVER DISEASE
items.statement: Immunosuppressive therapy is beneficial for all patients with established cirrhosis due to autoimmune hepatitis.

items.statement: Intrahepatic cholestatic processes will improve with surgical or endoscopic biliary tract decompression.

items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Biliary Cholangitis

items.statement: Plasmapheresis is a common treatment for pruritus in PBC.

items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Biliary Cholangitis

items.statement: The cause of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is well understood and linked to specific bacterial infections.

items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · PRIMARY SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS

items.statement: Patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) often present with elevated alkaline phosphatase levels.

items.statement: Patients with cardiac cirrhosis are likely to develop variceal hemorrhage.
items.explanation: It is unlikely that patients with cardiac cirrhosis will develop variceal hemorrhage.

items.reference: Cirrhosis and Its Complications · Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis | CARDIAC CIRRHOSIS

items.statement: Most individuals with the ZZ phenotype of $\alpha_{1}$AT deficiency will develop chronic liver disease.

items.subtopic: MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION
items.statement: Portal hypertension is primarily caused by prehepatic factors.
items.explanation: Intrahepatic causes account for >95% of cases of portal hypertension.

items.subtopic: MAJOR COMPLICATIONS OF CIRRHOSIS | PORTAL HYPERTENSION
items.statement: Patients with an HVPG >12 mmHg are at risk for variceal hemorrhage.

items.statement: Cerebral herniation can be a complication in acute liver failure due to brain edema.

items.statement: Dietary protein restriction is the mainstay of treatment for hepatic encephalopathy.

items.statement: Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) can be precipitated by systemic effects such as bacterial or fungal infection.

items.statement: Hepatopulmonary syndrome is often severe and requires immediate intervention.
items.explanation: HPS is often mild, though it can be treated with oxygen supplementation and liver transplantation.

items.statement: Patients with cirrhosis should consume 35 kcal/kg per day based on actual body weight.

items.assertion: Liver transplantation can be an effective long-term treatment for patients with alcoholic hepatitis.

items.assertion: Pruritus in PBC is usually most bothersome in the evening.

items.assertion: Cirrhosis is the most common cause of portal hypertension in the United States.

items.assertion: TIPS should be considered for patients who fail or are unable to receive endoscopic therapy.

items.reason: Ceftriaxone started prior to endoscopy reduces infections, recurrent bleeding, and mortality.

items.assertion: Portal hypertensive gastropathy can cause iron-deficiency anemia.

items.assertion: A SAAG <1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension as the cause of ascites.

items.assertion: The coagulation cascade in cirrhotic patients can easily tip toward thrombosis or bleeding.
